items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.
Students use magnetism as the test, but stainless steel (ferrous) can be non-magnetic. The defining property is iron content - 'ferrous' comes from the Latin for iron.

items.q: Which ISO standard governs Life Cycle Assessment principles?
items.why: It's ISO 14040. “ISO 9001” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 2002 Cradle to Cradle inspired circular design?
items.why: Correct answer: McDonough and Braungart. “Manzini and Vezzoli” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately what embodied energy does aluminium have (MJ per kg)?
items.why: Exact answer: 155 MJ/kg. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
